icd_category: C64 — Malignant neoplasm of kidney, except renal pelvis
one_liner: A 62-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with three months of intermittent painless gross hematuria, dull right flank pain, and a 15-pound unintentional weight loss.

stage2.hpi_extension: He describes 'tea-colored' urine on and off for about three months, which he initially ignored. Over the past month, he has noted a persistent dull ache in the right flank and early satiety. He has felt increasingly fatigued and has drenching night sweats. His wife noted a lump on the right side of his abdomen last week. He also reports a new scrotal heaviness on the left side that does not decompress when he lies down.
stage2.family_history: Father died of 'lung trouble' at 70. No known kidney disease.
stage2.social_history: Works as a boilermaker. 40-pack-year smoker, still smoking. Drinks 2 beers nightly.
stage2.physical_exam: General: thin, pale man appearing older than stated age. Abdomen: palpable firm mass in the right flank that does not cross midline; mild tenderness to deep palpation. Genitourinary: non-reducible left-sided varicocele that does not empty in the supine position. Skin: no rashes. Extremities: no edema. Lymph nodes: no cervical or supraclavicular adenopathy.

lab_results.result: Gross hematuria with >100 RBCs/hpf. No RBC casts. No dysmorphic RBCs. Protein trace. No pyuria, no nitrites.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Non-glomerular hematuria in an older smoker is highly concerning for urologic malignancy (renal or bladder) and mandates imaging and cystoscopy.

lab_results.result: Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (normal). Calcium 11.6 mg/dL (elevated). Albumin normal. LFTs mildly elevated (alk phos 180, ALT 55). Glucose normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hypercalcemia in the absence of bone metastases suggests paraneoplastic PTHrP secretion, a classic finding in renal cell carcinoma. Elevated alk phos raises concern for liver or bone involvement (Stauffer syndrome is a nonmetastatic paraneoplastic hepatic dysfunction).

lab_results.result: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast: 5.8 cm heterogeneously enhancing solid mass arising from the upper pole of the right kidney, with tumor thrombus extending into the right renal vein and IVC. Several enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes. No bony lesions.
lab_results.clinical_significance: An enhancing solid renal mass with renal vein/IVC invasion is diagnostic of renal cell carcinoma until proven otherwise. IVC tumor thrombus is a hallmark of RCC's propensity for venous extension.

stage5.final_clue: CBC reveals hemoglobin of 18.2 g/dL (polycythemia). He undergoes radical right nephrectomy with IVC thrombectomy. Pathology: clear cell renal cell carcinoma arising from proximal tubular epithelium, Fuhrman grade 3, with renal vein invasion.

debrief.classic_presentation: Renal cell carcinoma classically presents in men 50-70 years old, often smokers, with the classic triad of hematuria, flank pain, and a palpable flank mass — though this complete triad is seen in only about 10% of patients and usually indicates advanced disease. Constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fever, night sweats) are common. A left-sided varicocele that does not decompress in the supine position suggests obstruction of the left gonadal vein where it drains into the left renal vein by tumor thrombus. Paraneoplastic syndromes are a hallmark: EPO → polycythemia, PTHrP → hypercalcemia, renin → hypertension, and ACTH → Cushing syndrome. Stauffer syndrome is nonmetastatic hepatic dysfunction that resolves with tumor resection.
debrief.pathophysiology: RCC arises from the proximal convoluted tubule epithelium. The most common histologic subtype is clear cell carcinoma, driven by inactivation of the VHL tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 3p, which leads to constitutive stabilization of HIF and overproduction of VEGF, PDGF, and EPO. RCC is a tumor of fat-laden epithelial cells that spreads hematogenously, characteristically invading the renal vein and ascending the IVC to the right atrium. Common metastatic sites are lung ('cannonball' metastases) and bone. Risk factors: smoking, obesity, hypertension, acquired cystic disease on dialysis, and Von Hippel-Lindau disease (bilateral RCCs, pheochromocytoma, retinal/cerebellar hemangioblastomas).
debrief.treatment_overview: Localized disease: partial or radical nephrectomy is curative. Locally advanced disease with IVC thrombus: radical nephrectomy with thrombectomy. Metastatic disease: RCC is classically chemotherapy- and radiation-resistant. Targeted therapies (sunitinib, sorafenib, pazopanib — VEGF tyrosine kinase inhibitors) and mTOR inhibitors (temsirolimus, everolimus) are used, as are checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab). High-dose IL-2 was historically used in select fit patients.
high_yield_pearls: Classic triad of RCC = hematuria + flank pain + flank mass; present in only ~10% and usually indicates advanced disease.
Clear cell is the most common subtype, arising from proximal tubular epithelium; associated with VHL gene loss on chromosome 3p.
Left-sided varicocele that fails to decompress when lying down suggests left renal vein obstruction by tumor thrombus.
Paraneoplastic syndromes: EPO (polycythemia), PTHrP (hypercalcemia), renin (HTN), ACTH (Cushing). Stauffer syndrome = reversible hepatic dysfunction without liver metastases.
RCC invades the renal vein and IVC and classically metastasizes to lung ('cannonball' mets) and bone; it is chemo- and radiation-resistant, so resection is key.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Renal chapter, 'Renal cell carcinoma' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Renal chapter, 'Renal cyst disorders — Von Hippel-Lindau disease' (association with clear cell RCC).
